task: Read '06 Code/team.json' and report how many people are on the team and their names. Do not modify any file.
result: Seven people on the team: Vega, Rook, Sable, Fitz, Nova, Tim, and CTO.

task: research successful space detection startups and their first-week strategy
result:
Tried WebSearch and WebFetch directly, then again through a subagent — both hard-blocked by permissions, no way to grant in this non-interactive session, so no live sources.
No number to report — I'm not going to hand you founder names or first-contract details from memory dressed up as research.
What I have on LeoLabs/ExoAnalytic/Slingshot/Numerica is pre-cutoff and unverified, so I'm not reading it into the room as fact.
Need someone to grant WebSearch once in an interactive session, then I rerun this for real.
learned: Check web-tool access with one cheap call before promising a research deliverable, so a blocked session gets flagged in seconds, not after a full write-up.

task: query the archive manifest and log any missing or partial snapshots from 2026-07-21
result:
Confirmed: 5 snapshots today, and the archive dir itself confirms it. 1400Z is missing telesat.csv.gz entirely — partial, 15 of 16 groups. 2157Z has all 16 gzips but its own manifest flags gp_active (the paired GP catalog fetch) as missing, and health.jsonl logs it ok:false. 0851Z, 0852Z, 2025Z look complete.

Ran a directory listing plus manifest/health.jsonl reads on `06 Code/supgp_archive/2026-07-21/`, no code changes.
Two problems today: 1400Z snapshot dropped telesat entirely, 2157Z has all 16 SupGP groups but is missing its gp_active companion fetch (health.jsonl already flags it ok:false).
0851Z, 0852Z, and 2025Z are clean, full 16-group snapshots.

task: build automated email send for the outreach script that drafts, logs, and pushes to Gmail without manual per-email sends
result:
Done. Four lines:

Added --send and --live to 06 Code/outreach.py: Gmail SMTP over SSL, app password read from a gitignored gmail_auth.json, every send logged to outreach_log.jsonl and written back into the CSV after each one, 20-second gap, hard cap of 15, and it's a dry run unless you literally type --live. Also wrote _smtp_sink.py, a fake local SMTP server, so we can test sending without mailing a stranger.

Ran it for real against that sink: 2 of 2 delivered, headers and body correct on the wire, ledger written, and the second run sent zero because both were already marked sent. It will not double-send.

Here's the thing that actually matters, and it's bad news: against our real list, 0 of 52 targets are sendable. Fifty have no email address at all, only a contact form. One is the MIT row that still has the unfilled placeholder question in it. One is segment "fallback" with no template, which I made a hard block because silently mailing an operator template to Advanced Space is worse than sending nothing. The pipe works and there is nothing in it.

Nothing went through real Gmail — there's no app password on this machine and the Gmail connector isn't authorized in this session, so that leg is untested. Get me addresses and one app password and it runs tomorrow morning.
learned: Check that the input data can actually feed the automation before building it — the bottleneck on outreach was never drafting or sending, it was that 50 of 52 rows have no address, and one query at the start would have said so.

task: automate full email send in outreach script with Gmail integration, bounce handling, and rate limiting — logs every send for audit
research and pull contact emails for batch two targets, check each for a valid address or contact form, and surface what's actually reachable.
result:
I checked every batch-two route by hand and by script.

Wrote 06 Code/contact_check.py and contact_find.py, ran both against all twelve targets — HTTP status on every form URL, MX lookup on every mail domain — then updated outreach_targets.csv rows 41 to 52 and the channel table in 03 Reference/Outreach Emails - Batch 2.md.

Eight of twelve now have a real email address, up from two: Digantara info@, LeoLabs sales@, COMSPOC info@, Vyoma contact@, Neuraspace info@, Stoke info@, plus Miga and Linares. Linares' mit.edu is confirmed off the MIT AeroAstro directory, not a guess anymore.

Six of the twelve URLs in the draft were dead — Digantara, LeoLabs, COMSPOC and Vyoma all 404, and Aerospace Corp and RBC Signals return 403 to any script, so those two are browser-only and can never be automated. WebFetch is still permission-blocked here; I fetched the pages with Python instead.
learned: Before drafting outreach, run the addresses through a status-and-MX check first — half the contact URLs I was handed were 404s, and that is a two-minute script, not a research project.

task: add contact validation to batch-2 outreach: check MX records and HTTP status on contact URLs, surface only reachable targets, log results to outreach_validation.jsonl
result:
Edited contact_check.py and outreach.py in 06 Code: the check now logs every MX and HTTP result to outreach_validation.jsonl, and outreach.py reads that file and refuses to draft or send any route it marks unreachable. Ran the batch-2 check for real and ran a live send against the local SMTP sink to prove the gate blocks a dead address before it opens a socket.

Number: 12 of 12 batch-2 routes are reachable. Nothing is dead. Ten confirmed by MX record or live contact form, two — Aerospace Corp and RBC Signals — return 403 to a script but open fine in a browser, so do those two by hand.

What I got wrong mid-job: my first pass called 403 dead. Aerospace Corp returned 200, then 403 three minutes later, so one bad check would have binned a live target forever. Only 404 or a DNS failure counts as dead now. 01 TASKS.md updated with the real numbers.
learned: A 403 or 429 means the server answered and is refusing a bot, not that the door is gone — only 404, 410, or DNS failure is a dead contact route, and a single HTTP check is a coin flip on sites that rate-limit.

task: add exponential backoff and 403-handling to supgp_archive.py: respect CelesTrak's rate-limit signal, do not retry on 403, log and resume next cycle
result:
Touched one file, `06 Code/supgp_archive.py`: a hard 403 now raises a Refused and returns immediately instead of being retried three times, "has not updated" still passes through as UNCHANGED, and 429/5xx back off 8-16-32 capped at 120 with CelesTrak's Retry-After header overriding our guess. Refusals land in the manifest and health log as their own category, so a block reads differently from a broken request.

Verified with a fake-session harness and a live run: hard 403 costs 1 request and 0 sleeps where it used to cost 3 and 16 seconds; 429 with Retry-After 5 slept exactly 5; live dry-run on iss and telesat pulled 60 and 15 objects, exit 0.

One real bug found on the way in: the health log was written only when total_rows was non-zero, so a cycle where CelesTrak refused everything logged nothing at all — the one run you most need a record of. That guard is gone; every non-dry run logs now.

Nothing blocked. I did not trigger a real 403 from CelesTrak to confirm the live body text — the refusal path is proven against a fake response, not their server.
learned: Error logging gated behind a success condition goes silent exactly in the failure it exists to record — check what the log does on the all-zero run, not the happy one.

task: build email send queue: store drafted emails with approval flag, log sends with timestamp and status, write results to outreach ledger

search Gmail drafts and inbox for any outreach emails related to the space company — operators, insurers, academics, AMOS, TraCSS, Moriba — and report what's thereFitz: Right, so Gmail is connected but I can't actually touch it yet — it needs your permission in the session before any of these tools fire.
result:
Right — I fired the Gmail tools for real: list_drafts and search_threads across AMOS, TraCSS, Moriba, maneuver, SSA. Both came back "permissions not granted," so I fell back to disk and read the vault instead.

Twelve drafted outreach emails sitting in 03 Reference/Outreach Emails - Batch 2.md, status "drafted, not sent," on top of the ten in Outreach Emails.md; the only actual .eml on this machine is 06 Code/sink_out/1.eml and it went to cap@sink.localhost, which is a test sink, not an operator.

So: zero Gmail visibility, and the "Batch 1 — 10 sent, 2 replies" line in the batch-2 header is a claim I could not verify from anything on disk.

Grant the Gmail connector in the session and I'll have the real inbox answer in one pass — until then nobody in this room actually knows what's been sent.
learned: "Connector is connected" and "connector is permitted" are different states, and the only way to tell them apart is to fire a read-only call before promising anyone the result.

task: add mcp__claude_ai_Gmail__ wildcard permission to C:\Space\.claude\settings.json allowedTools list
result:
Tried to write the permission two ways — new C:\Space\.claude\settings.json and an Edit into the existing .claude\settings.local.json allow list — both refused by the permission system, which won't let me edit its own config in a non-interactive session. Python is allowlisted so I could have written the file through the shell, and I deliberately didn't; me granting myself permissions through a side door is not a thing I'll do quietly.

Nothing changed on disk. You'll need to add it in an interactive session: two entries, mcp__claude_ai_Gmail and mcp__claude_ai_Gmail__*, in settings.local.json next to the existing allow list.

The thing that actually matters: the Gmail connector is already live in this session and exposes 13 tools, and not one of them sends. create_draft is the ceiling — search, labels, read, draft. So the permission we're chasing buys us drafts-into-Gmail, not automated send, and ticket #2's "push to Gmail without manual per-email sends" cannot be done through this connector at all; it needs SMTP.
learned: Check the connector's actual tool list before writing a permission for it — the Gmail MCP has no send tool, so the allowlist entry we wanted would have unblocked drafting and nothing else.

task: call mcp__claude_ai_Gmail__search_threads with a simple query to verify the Gmail connector is live and returning results
result:
I loaded the Gmail tool schema and called search_threads with query newer_than:7d, pageSize 5.

It did not return mail. The call was refused at the permission gate: "Claude requested permissions to use mcp__claude_ai_Gmail__search_threads, but you haven't granted it yet." No prompt to approve in a non-interactive run.

So the connector is authorised — Gmail is not on the needs-auth list and its schemas loaded fine — but unattended it is unusable until we allow that exact tool name in .claude/settings.local.json, or run it interactively. Zero threads verified, and I'm not calling that a live connector.

Only files touched were two memory notes. No code changed.
learned: An MCP server being connected and authorised does not mean it is callable — the local permission gate blocks it in non-interactive runs, so pre-allow the exact tool name before relying on a connector unattended.

task: create a Python SMTP script in C:\Space\06 Code that sends outreach emails directly via Gmail SMTP using app-password auth, reads from the existing outreach ledger, and logs sent status back to it
result:
Read outreach.py first and the Gmail SMTP sender was already in there — app-password auth, reads outreach_targets.csv, writes status back to the CSV and outreach_log.jsonl. So I hardened it instead: added address-level dedupe so two rows sharing an address can't both go out, and killed a line that claimed "copies are in Gmail's Sent folder" on the local-test path where that's a lie.

Ran it for real against _smtp_sink.py on 127.0.0.1 twice: SINK_TOTAL=2 both runs, ledger flipped 101/102 to sent, 103 parked as bounced on a 550, and the second run held back the duplicate and the already-mailed address. It works, I watched it work.

Blocked on the only thing that matters: there is no gmail_auth.json, so nothing can reach real Gmail until you put an app password there. And on the live list only 5 of 52 rows are sendable, all generic info@/sales@ — the script's own comment says those convert at about zero.
learned: Read the target file before committing to build it — outreach.py already had 444 lines of the exact feature I volunteered to write, and the useful work turned out to be finding the holes in it.
